name: Zucchini and hazelnut salad
intro: want to roast them as I describe, or keep fresh. With or without hazelnuts, this makes the most luxurious

ingredients.item: olive oil salt and black pepper
mixed green and purple basil leaves
top-quality Parmesan, broken up or very thinly sliced

method: Preheat the oven to 300°F. Scatter the hazelnuts over a baking sheet and roast for 12 to 15 minutes, or until nicely browned. Let them cool down before chopping roughly or just crushing lightly with the side of a large knife.
Place a ridged griddle pan on a high heat and leave it there until it’s almost red-hot – at least 5 minutes.
Meanwhile, trim the ends of the zucchini and cut them on an angle into

³8-inch-thick slices. Place them in a bowl and toss with half the olive oil and some salt and pepper. Place the slices in the hot grill pan and char-grill for about 2 minutes on each side; turn them over using tongs. You want to get distinct char marks without cooking the zucchini through. Transfer to a mixing bowl, pour over the balsamic vinegar, toss together and set aside.

Once the zucchini have cooled down, add the remaining olive oil, the basil and hazelnuts. Mix lightly, then taste and adjust the seasoning accordingly. Transfer the salad to a flat plate, incorporating the Parmesan, and drizzle over the hazelnut oil.

You can use most varieties of pumpkin for these satisfying wedges. Serve with Green Gazpacho (page 180) to make a light and healthy-feeling supper. They will also make a perfect veggie main course for Christmas as the crust is a bit like stuffing.
Crusted pumpkin wedges with sour cream
